title: _redirects: 301 /counselling → https://alexmarshallcounselling.com
description: Add a _redirects rule mapping /counselling on alxm.me → https://alexmarshallcounselling.com (301). Purpose: anyone who types or shares 'alxm.me/counselling' (rather than clicking the nav) still lands on the counselling site. Pairs with the nav-href bead — together they give both browse-path and direct-URL coverage. Sequencing: do not enable until the alexmarshallcounselling.com domain is serving traffic. Part 2 of 2 for coord bead setup-defaults-rtv.5.

title: Update Counselling nav entry HREF to https://alexmarshallcounselling.com
description: Add (or update) a 'Counselling' entry in site.nav (src/_data/site.js) whose href is the external URL https://alexmarshallcounselling.com. Clicking the nav goes straight to the counselling site — no on-domain route required for the click path. Pair with the _redirects bead so /counselling on alxm.me also lands at the same place when typed or shared directly. Verify the nav renders with the entry, ordered sensibly relative to existing items. Part 1 of 2 for coord bead setup-defaults-rtv.5.

title: _redirects: 301 old /writing/<psych-slug>/ URLs → alexmarshallcounselling.com
description: Add 301 redirects in _redirects (or an equivalent Worker rule / CF zone-level rule) so existing indexed alxm.me/writing/<psychology-slug>/ URLs resolve to https://alexmarshallcounselling.com/writing/<slug>/. Catches bookmarks, search-engine entries, and inbound links that pre-date the cutover. Slug list should be sourced from the current set of psychology-tagged articles; consider a build step that emits the redirect entries from KV data so the list stays in sync as new psychology articles ship. Sequencing: do not enable until the alexmarshallcounselling.com domain is serving traffic (sister epic setup-defaults-67b cutover) — otherwise redirects target a 404. Part 3 of 3 for coord bead setup-defaults-rtv.2.

title: Suppress local page generation for psychology-tagged articles
description: Stop rendering /writing/<psychology-slug>/ pages on alxm.me — the KV-sourced item still exists in the data layer, but the per-page output is filtered out at build time. Likely a pagination.before or eleventyComputed filter in src/writing/writing.11tydata.js (mirroring the pattern alexmarshall.me uses inversely to filter to-only-psychology). Verify after the change that (1) a non-psychology article still builds normally, (2) a psychology article no longer has a local /writing/<slug>/ output, (3) the listing still surfaces the entry per the bead above. Part 2 of 3 for coord bead setup-defaults-rtv.2.

title: Render psychology-tagged listing entries with external HREF + external-link arrow indicator
description: On the /writing/ listing page (and any tag archive pages that include psychology), render psychology-tagged items as external-link entries: title + summary visible as today, but the entry link points to https://alexmarshallcounselling.com/writing/<slug>/ instead of the local /writing/<slug>/ URL. Add a small external-link arrow indicator (e.g. ↗) next to the title or summary so visitors aren't surprised by leaving the domain when they click. Non-psychology items render as today (local link to local article). Implementation likely in src/_includes/partials/writing-collection.liquid (and/or article.liquid) keyed off item.tags including 'psychology'. Part 1 of 3 for coord bead setup-defaults-rtv.2.

title: [epic] Counselling nav entry on alxm.me (coord: alexmarshall.me rtv.5)
description: Add a 'Counselling' entry to alxm.me's nav bar as the primary handoff path to the counselling site. Two complementary changes: (1) the nav entry's href points externally to https://alexmarshallcounselling.com so a click from the nav leaves the domain directly; (2) a _redirects rule maps /counselling on alxm.me → https://alexmarshallcounselling.com so anyone who types or shares 'alxm.me/counselling' lands in the same place. Both ship together. Sequencing: around or after the counselling domain is live (sister epic setup-defaults-67b cutover). Cross-repo coordinator: setup-defaults-rtv.5.

title: [epic] Writing pipeline split — psychology articles move to counselling site (coord: alexmarshall.me rtv.2)
description: Reshape alxm.me's writing pipeline so psychology-tagged articles become source-of-truth on alexmarshallcounselling.com. Three concerns: (1) /writing/ listing renders psychology items with title + summary but external HREF (https://alexmarshallcounselling.com/writing/<slug>/) and an external-link arrow indicator so visitors aren't surprised when they leave the domain; (2) page generation for psychology-tagged articles is suppressed on alxm.me — the KV item still exists, just isn't rendered as a local page; (3) existing indexed alxm.me/writing/<psychology-slug>/ URLs 301 → alexmarshallcounselling.com/writing/<slug>/ via _redirects (or Worker rule) so external links and search-index entries continue to resolve. Sequencing: ship around or after the alexmarshallcounselling.com domain is live (sister epic setup-defaults-67b cutover). Cross-repo coordinator: setup-defaults-rtv.2.

title: Migrate project-defaults updates: CLAUDE/AGENTS session-close + missing bd memories
description:
Apply two items from the project-defaults snapshot to alxm.me:

1. Update CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md Session Completion sections to drop the mandatory 'bd dolt push' step (already documented as no-op in bd-persistence memory for this project) and add the auto-delete-on-merge cleanup guidance from the new CLAUDE.md.template.

2. Seed 12 missing bd memories from project-defaults/beads/default-memories.md:
   alex-never-push-to-main, gh-auto-delete-on-merge, alex-pr-review-style, alex-large-pr-honesty, named-constants-and-dry, pattern-coverage-across-peers, rename-sweep, test-coverage-discipline, bd-multi-line-writes, bd-tagging-conventions, bead-authoring-scope, bd-data-grooming-protocol, task-tracking-policy

Scope limited per user request — skipping FUNDING.yml, git-sync-branches.sh, .gitignore tweaks, package.json polish.

title: PR #208 follow-up tweaks: subscribe form UX polish from visual review
description:
Follow-ups identified during visual review of PR #208 (subscribe form UX improvements). Five issues to address:

1. **Flow space before subscribe-container is too small.** The .subscribe-form block sets --flow-space: 2xs on itself, which the parent .flow composition reads when computing margin-block-start for the form (CSS custom property inheritance). Result: subscribe form gets ~11px margin instead of the article's m-sized flow space (~33px). Fix: set --flow-space on the form's children (.flow descendants), not on the form element itself.

2. **CTA copy change.** Update default CTA from 'Enjoyed this? Get new posts by email.' to 'Did you enjoy this? Get new posts by email.' Touchpoints: src/_includes/partials/subscribe-form.liquid default value, and the override passed from article.liquid.

3. **Box width and form-on-new-line.** Article-end subscribe-container should match the measure of article text (likely measure 'long' = 65ch — confirm against article styling). Form fields should render on the line after the CTA, not be horizontally squeezed inside the box.

4. **switcher-2 fails on desktop, shows a single column.** Every Layout's switcher composition has a 'no more than N across' rule that collapses to full-column when child count exceeds max-items. With 4 children and max-items=2, it goes column instead of 2x2. Fix: replace .switcher-2 with the grid composition (sets --min-item-width: measure-compact) — wraps at the right breakpoint without the all-column failure mode. Remove .switcher-2 from compositions if no other consumers.

5. **Contact card subscribe button doesn't match contact-options__link styling.** Default .button block has padding: 2xs and radius applied; contact-options__link has padding-block: 2xs only and no radius. Inside .contact-options the subscribe button should match the link's shape (padding/corners) and hover behaviour exactly so all four cards present consistent visual weight.

All five touch CSS/SCSS — invoke the cube-css skill before changing styles.
close_reason: Closed

title: Remove podcast references from landing page, contact page, and any other surfaces
description:
Companion to alxm_me-odj.1 (which hides the nav entry only). Even with the nav hidden, the podcast is still surfaced on:

- The home/landing page (any podcast block, recent episodes, CTA, etc.)
- The contact page (contact-options entries that point to podcast platforms or the show)
- Any other place that links to /podcast/ or a podcast platform from non-podcast pages (footer, about, sidebar, etc.)

Keep these resources LIVE:
- /podcast/ landing page
- Individual episode pages
- RSS feed
- R2-hosted MP3s

The goal is to stop promoting the podcast from non-podcast surfaces while keeping all canonical podcast URLs resolvable so subscribers, YouTube descriptions, and inbound links don't break.

Acceptance: grep src/ for podcast references after the change — only podcast collection pages, the data file, and the worker's R2 routing should remain. No non-podcast page renders a link/promo/CTA pointing at podcast content.

title: Hide Podcast nav entry in src/_data/site.js
description: Remove or conditionally render the 'Podcast' entry in site.nav (src/_data/site.js) so it no longer appears in the header nav bar. Leave /podcast/, individual episode pages, the Atom/RSS feed, and R2-hosted MP3s untouched — the page tree remains live, only the navigation surface changes. Verify after the change that direct visits to /podcast/, an episode URL, and the feed still resolve in dev. Closes coord bead setup-defaults-rtv.1 once shipped.

title: [epic] Podcast retirement on alxm.me (coord: alexmarshall.me rtv.1, rtv.4)
description: Hide the Podcast nav entry on alxm.me while keeping /podcast/, individual episode pages, RSS feed, and R2-hosted MP3s live (so podcast-app subscribers, YouTube descriptions, and inbound links keep resolving). Cross-repo coordinator beads on the sister side: setup-defaults-rtv.1 (hide nav) and setup-defaults-rtv.4 (post-change QA). Close those coordinators on the alexmarshall.me side once children here ship.

title: Stop blowing through GitHub LFS quota on every Cloudflare preview build
description:
Every commit on every branch triggers a Cloudflare Workers Builds preview, which spins up a fresh ephemeral environment that re-downloads all LFS objects from GitHub. With many branches/commits per month, this exhausts the GitHub LFS bandwidth quota.

Goal: investigate options to stop preview builds from consuming LFS bandwidth, or move LFS-tracked content out of the preview-build path entirely.

Options to evaluate (non-exhaustive):

1. **Skip LFS download in preview builds.** Configure the Cloudflare build to use `GIT_LFS_SKIP_SMUDGE=1` so LFS pointers aren't resolved during checkout. The build can still succeed if LFS-tracked files aren't needed for the static-site build (they may already be excluded from Eleventy passthrough and synced to R2 separately).

2. **Move LFS content off LFS.** Audit what's tracked by LFS today (`git lfs ls-files`). Large files that are already R2-synced may not need to live in git at all — could be uploaded directly to R2 and referenced by URL, removing them from the repo entirely.

3. **Switch from the Cloudflare repo link to a GitHub Actions workflow.** Build in Actions (which has its own LFS handling and can cache or skip), then deploy to Cloudflare via wrangler. Trades the auto-PR-preview convenience for full control over the build environment and LFS access.

4. **Skip preview builds for non-deploy branches.** Configure Workers Builds to only build production/staging branches, not every push. Loses preview-URL-per-PR but eliminates the per-commit LFS hit.

5. **Self-host LFS / migrate to a different LFS backend.** e.g. Cloudflare R2 as LFS storage via a proxy, or a different provider with a more generous quota. More invasive.

Pick whichever combination minimizes LFS bandwidth without losing preview-per-PR if possible. Option 1 is the cheapest experiment — try it first.

Acceptance: monthly LFS bandwidth usage stays well under the quota with normal branching/commit activity.
close_reason: PR #228: build now resilient to GIT_LFS_SKIP_SMUDGE=1 (audio-validation skips pointer files, R2 sync refuses to upload them). Operational follow-up: set GIT_LFS_SKIP_SMUDGE=1 as a Cloudflare Workers Builds env var for preview branches in the dashboard.

title: Resolve 6 Dependabot alerts: undici <7.24.0 (transitive via miniflare) in _cloudflare/r2
description:
GitHub Dependabot has flagged 6 vulnerabilities against `undici` in `_cloudflare/r2/pnpm-lock.yaml`. All 6 advisories share the same vulnerable range (`>=7.0.0, <7.24.0`) and same patched version (`7.24.0`), so they collapse to one fix decision and one upgrade.

Currently locked: `undici@7.18.2`, pulled in transitively as `wrangler@4.59.1` → `miniflare@4.20260128.0` → `undici@7.18.2`. Not a direct dependency.

## Alerts

| # | Severity | CVE | Summary |
|---|---|---|---|
| [#39](https://github.com/alexmensch/alxm.me/security/dependabot/39) | high | CVE-2026-1526 | Unbounded Memory Consumption in WebSocket permessage-deflate Decompression |
| [#38](https://github.com/alexmensch/alxm.me/security/dependabot/38) | high | CVE-2026-2229 | Unhandled Exception in WebSocket Client (invalid `server_max_window_bits`) |
| [#35](https://github.com/alexmensch/alxm.me/security/dependabot/35) | high | CVE-2026-1528 | Malicious WebSocket 64-bit length overflow crashes client |
| [#37](https://github.com/alexmensch/alxm.me/security/dependabot/37) | medium | CVE-2026-1527 | CRLF Injection via `upgrade` option |
| [#36](https://github.com/alexmensch/alxm.me/security/dependabot/36) | medium | CVE-2026-2581 | Unbounded Memory Consumption in `DeduplicationHandler` (DoS) |
| [#34](https://github.com/alexmensch/alxm.me/security/dependabot/34) | medium | CVE-2026-1525 | HTTP Request/Response Smuggling |

## Decision needed

**Fix or dismiss?** Reachability is low — `undici` here is only used by `miniflare` for local Workers emulation during `wrangler` operations in the R2 sync script (`_cloudflare/r2/`). The script does not run a long-lived server, does not accept untrusted WebSocket connections, and only runs in CI/dev to push files to R2. Most of the high-severity advisories require attacker-controlled WebSocket traffic, which we don't expose. So strict exploitability here is near zero.

That said, dismissing 6 alerts (3 high, 3 medium) is noisier than just bumping. Recommendation: **fix**, because the upgrade is cheap.

## Fix options

1. **Bump `wrangler`** (`4.59.1` → `4.87.0`). Latest `miniflare@4.20260430.0` ships `undici@7.24.8` transitively. Cleanest — root-cause fix, no override drift, picks up other miniflare/wrangler fixes too. Risk: minor wrangler API changes; verify `_cloudflare/r2/` scripts still work end-to-end.
2. **`pnpm.overrides`** (add `"undici": ">=7.24.0"` next to existing `fast-xml-parser` override in `_cloudflare/r2/package.json`). Surgical, isolates the change to one transitive package. Use if option 1 introduces wrangler regressions.

Prefer option 1; fall back to option 2 if wrangler bump misbehaves.

## Cross-repo

Sister repo `alexmensch/alexmarshall.me` has the identical 6 alerts at the same `_cloudflare/r2/pnpm-lock.yaml` and same `wrangler` version. Whichever fix is chosen here should be mirrored there in the same PR cycle. Tracking only here per "do the work once" guidance — alexmarshall.me has its own beads instance so a separate issue there would just duplicate this one.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] All 6 Dependabot alerts on alxm.me are resolved (auto-closed by Dependabot once `pnpm-lock.yaml` reflects `undici >=7.24.0`)
- [ ] R2 sync (`pnpm run build:cf` or equivalent) still succeeds with the upgraded dependencies
- [ ] Same fix applied to `alexmensch/alexmarshall.me`

title: titleCaseTag helper + per-tag Atom feeds
description: (1) Add titleCaseTag function to src/_data/helpers.js — splits hyphenated tag (e.g. personal-growth) on hyphens and title-cases each word; registered as a Liquid filter. Frontmatter raw value stays lowercase. (2) New src/writing-tag-feeds.liquid + src/writing-tag-feeds.11tydata.js: paginates over unique tags, generates /writing/{tag-slug}.atom for each. Same Atom 1.0 entry format as the existing /writing.atom — extracted to shared partial src/_includes/partials/atom-entry.liquid (used by both feeds). Feed metadata reflects the tag name. Existing /writing.atom unchanged (still includes all writing articles).

title: Per-tag phrase mapping (src/_data/tagPhrases.js)
description: New src/_data/tagPhrases.js — an object mapping tag string to phrase template (e.g. psychology → 'From the annals of {tag}'). Entries already populated for the existing tags in the writing corpus: art, aviation, essay, fiction, financial, nature, software, plus psychology. Validation plugin enforces every tag has an entry; build fails otherwise (see tag-validation task).

title: New Yorker–style article flourish (with linked tag)
description: On individual article pages, render an editorial category flourish using a per-tag phrase mapping (e.g. 'From the annals of Psychology'). The tag name within the phrase is a link to /writing/{tag-slug}/. Modify src/_includes/layouts/posts.liquid and src/_includes/partials/article.liquid. New tagPhrase filter looks up the phrase template and substitutes a {tag} placeholder with the title-cased linked tag. Flourish uses the article's first tag only. New .article__flourish styles in src/assets/scss/blocks/_article.scss: sans-serif, small, uppercase, letter-spaced, visually secondary.

title: Tag archive pages at /writing/{tag-slug}/
description: New src/writing/tags.liquid + src/writing/tags.11tydata.js. Paginates over unique tags from the writing collection (using shared helpers.getUniqueTags()). Generates /writing/{tag-slug}/ for every tag that exists on a published article. Uses layouts/writing.liquid; hero title 'Writing: {Tag Name}' (title-cased). Filtering by array containment (tags.includes(tag)) using a Liquid loop with 'contains'. Tag slug uses helpers.toSlug (URL-safe lowercase hyphenated).

title: Pills link to tag archive pages
description: Each category pill on the writing collection list is a native anchor element to /writing/{tag-slug}/, matching the archive page route from the related task. Pill is a standard <a>, keyboard-focusable. Uses the toSlug filter (registered in .eleventy.js — exposes existing helpers.toSlug as a Liquid filter).

title: Curated tag colour palette + tagColorIndex helper (light + dark mode)
description: In src/assets/scss/global/_variables.scss, add 8–12 --tag-color-N CSS custom properties at :root and override in @media (prefers-color-scheme: dark). Derive from existing colour families. Each colour meets WCAG AA 3:1 contrast against its mode's background. Add tagColorIndex helper to src/_data/helpers.js (simple string hash modulo TAG_PALETTE_SIZE) and register as a Liquid filter. Same tag → same colour. Cross-reference comment on TAG_PALETTE_SIZE pointing to _variables.scss.

title: Category pill on writing collection list
description: Modify src/_includes/partials/writing-collection.liquid: add pill <a> next to the date for each article entry. Each pill shows the article's first tag in title case, sans-serif font, thin outline (no fill) using inline style --pill-color CSS var. New SCSS block src/assets/scss/blocks/_category-pill.scss with border-radius: 100vw, border: 1px solid var(--pill-color), small font size, max-width: 20ch with text-overflow: ellipsis to handle long tag names. Pill is keyboard-accessible.

title: Tag validation plugin + tagPhrases enforcement
description: New eleventy-plugins/tag-validation.js (following audio-validation.js pattern). Exports testable validateWritingTags(collection, tagPhrases). Each article in writing collection must have at least one tag (frontmatter 'tags' array). Every tag used in published articles must have a corresponding entry in src/_data/tagPhrases.js. Build fails with a clear, file-named error otherwise. Validation runs after KV plugin populates the collection (eleventy.after / addCollection callback). Validation is invoked from src/writing/tags.11tydata.js (no plugin wrapper).

title: Add category indicators to writing collection (PR #211)
description:
Surface article categories throughout the writing section: pill-shaped tag labels on the collection list (linking to per-tag archive pages), per-tag Atom feeds, and a New Yorker–style editorial flourish on individual article pages. Build-time validation enforces every article has at least one tag and every tag has a phrase mapping.

Source: previously documented in product/category-indicators.md (now removed). Implementation lives on branch claude/add-category-indicators-6mStC — see PR #211 for review.

Out-of-spec changes already made on the branch: shared getUniqueTags helper, shared atom-entry partial, multi-tag support (single tag → tags array), frontmatter rename to standard 'tags' field, and tagPhrases entries for art/aviation/essay/fiction/financial/nature/software.

Files added: eleventy-plugins/tag-validation.js, src/_data/tagPhrases.js, src/assets/scss/blocks/_category-pill.scss, src/writing/tags.{liquid,11tydata.js}, src/writing-tag-feeds.{liquid,11tydata.js}, src/_includes/partials/atom-entry.liquid.

Files modified: .eleventy.js, src/_data/helpers.js, src/_includes/partials/writing-collection.liquid, src/_includes/layouts/posts.liquid, src/_includes/partials/article.liquid, src/assets/scss/global/_variables.scss, src/assets/scss/blocks/_article.scss, src/assets/scss/blocks/_index.scss, src/writing/writing.md, src/writing-atom-feed.liquid.

title: Add switcher-2 composition class + apply to contact options
description: In src/assets/scss/compositions/_switcher.scss, add a .switcher-2 class that uses the existing switcher mixin with $max-items: 2 and the same $min-item-width as .switcher (dt.get-value('measure', 'compact')). Then in src/_includes/partials/contact-options.liquid, change the container class from [ switcher ] to [ switcher-2 ] so the 4 option cards render 2x2 on wide viewports and stack on narrow. Existing .switcher (3-item) stays unchanged.

Specificity scoped to .contact-options .subscribe-form so article-page styling is unaffected.

The episode media player (type == 'episode') continues to render before content. Acceptance: subscribe form HTML appears after article body; subscription guard preserved; short content still shows form below.

title: Improve subscribe form UX (PR #208)

Reposition the email subscribe form below article content (instead of above), reduce its visual weight, soften CTA copy, and add a subscribe option card to the contact page. Also introduces a switcher-2 CSS composition for the resulting 2x2 contact-options layout.

Source: previously documented in product/improve-subscribe-form-ux.md (now removed). Implementation lives on branch claude/improve-subscribe-form-ux-i5xYE — see PR #208 for review.

All CSS changes go through the cube-css skill (CUBE CSS conventions, design tokens).

value:
PR review style — when Alex asks to review a PR before merging.

Persona: engineering manager reviewing code; demanding, precise, strict.

What to evaluate (in priority order):
1. Coding elegance — no brute-force approaches, no jank
2. DRY — keeping things DRY is paramount; flag duplicated logic, magic numbers, parallel implementations across files
3. Bigger architectural picture — not just the changed lines; how the change fits the codebase's existing patterns and conventions
4. Tests — identify tests that need updating or adding

Output format: concise report with what should be changed and why, then GET APPROVAL before proceeding with any fixes. Do NOT start fixing until Alex agrees with the findings.

Out-of-scope findings: file as bd issues under the project's active code-quality epic (if one exists; create one if not and the project has accumulated 3+ such findings). Don't lose architectural debt to chat-only mentions.

How to apply: when invoked via "review PR N" / "review this branch" / similar phrasing, fetch the PR diff (gh pr view, gh pr diff), read the files in full not just the hunks (to evaluate architectural fit), check both the changed code and adjacent code paths the change interacts with, scan tests for coverage gaps, then deliver findings without unsolicited fixes.

Alex's GitHub repos have auto-delete-on-merge enabled in repo settings: feature branches are deleted from the remote automatically when a PR merges.

How to apply: cleanup after merge is local-only. Run `git fetch --prune`, then `git worktree remove` for the worktree and `git branch -D` for the local branch. Do NOT run `git push --delete origin <branch>`; the remote-side delete already happened and the command will fail or no-op.

If a specific project ever turns this off, the symptom is `git fetch --prune` not removing the merged branch on the remote — at that point either re-enable in repo settings or add the explicit `git push --delete` to that project's session-close.

Two paired rules to prevent magic-number / DRY findings at code-review time. These rules OVERRIDE the Claude Code system prompt's "Three similar lines is better than a premature abstraction" / "a bug fix doesn't need surrounding cleanup" defaults. Project CLAUDE.md should restate the override so it survives system-prompt drift.

1. Hoist numeric literals to named consts at first sight of a second usage. Any literal that is referenced in more than one place — or that encodes a tuned/calibrated value (pixel thresholds, mag-biases, near/far clamps, bit positions) — gets a named export at its canonical source module. Tests IMPORT it, never redefine. If a literal is calibrated by feel, it MUST be named — the name documents intent.

2. Schemas / structures / functions that are mostly-identical share a builder. When v2 and v3 of a wire schema differ only in 4 of 20 entries, or two materials differ only in their blend equation, or two parsers differ only in the projection of fields, or two solvers differ only in tolerance and wrap convention — extract a builder/factory/helper and parameterise the differences. Verbatim duplication of large blocks is the strongest signal of latent drift. "Slightly different X and Y" between two call sites is the case FOR extracting, not against it — the difference becomes a parameter.

Why: Code-review patterns consistently surface DRY / magic-number findings. The cost of one shared module beats the cost of (a) future drift, (b) review burden when each consumer must be cross-checked, and (c) tests redefining canonical constants and silently desyncing.

How to apply:
1. When typing a numeric literal, pause and ask: is this value (or the same dimension — e.g. another pixel-distance threshold, another bit position, another empty-vec3 default) referenced anywhere else? If yes, hoist to a const in the most upstream module.
2. When two functions / constants / schemas share more than ~50% of their bodies, ask whether the differences are parameters of one shared definition. If yes, extract the builder.
3. Tests: never redefine a constant the production code exports. Import it.
4. Comment-DRY counts too: if the same caveat appears verbatim in two consumer files, hoist the comment to the source helper.

Common misattribution to watch for: if you find yourself drafting reasoning like "Lifting it to a shared module is tempting but each call site has slightly different X and Y. Default: copy-paste with attribution comment, lift later only if a third call site appears. Avoids premature abstraction per CLAUDE.md" — STOP. That reasoning is wrong. "Slightly different X and Y" between two call sites is the trigger for rule 2 above — parameterise the differences and extract. The two-call-site threshold is firm. Do the extract; pass the differing values as parameters.

When a PR renames or removes an API surface (function, method, event, class, mechanism, or named threshold), grep the entire repo for the OLD names AND for prose descriptions of the old behaviour, and update both in the SAME commit. Specifically: code comments referencing removed methods; docs/*.md describing removed mechanisms; citations and numerical sanity-checks in reference docs; inline contracts on type declarations; perf-hud / telemetry marker labels; HTML <title> / modal copy; release-notes / version-classification when behaviour changes.

Why: recurring "comment / doc / marker still says old name" finding shape. No test catches them; the next reader is misled. Representative shapes: perf marker `onFrame.total` after the event was renamed to `frame`; a doc trade-off paragraph describing a mechanism that the same PR removed.

How to apply: before commit, treat the rename as a sweep, not just a refactor.
1. `grep -rn "<old-name>" .` (skip node_modules, .git, public/) — triage every hit.
2. Open every doc file in the diff context (docs/*.md, README.md, CLAUDE.md, RELEASING.md, project reference docs) and re-read it as if seeing it the first time. Stale prose is the most common drift class.
3. When changing the semantics of a quantity referenced in a docblock, open the docblock and re-read its rationale. If the change invalidates the prose, update the prose.
4. Numerical sanity-check examples in docs need to be sanity-checked themselves — paste-compute them, do not trust the original author.
5. Release-notes / version classification: a user-visible behaviour change is at minimum a minor bump even if the diff is small.

When diagnosing CSS/layout issues, do not confidently propose root causes from SCSS source reasoning alone. ch units, fluid clamp() values, container widths, and computed styles all resolve at runtime and depend on actual font metrics — estimates by inspection are often off by 30-50%.

Why: Alex called this out explicitly. Earlier in PR #208's follow-up, I confidently diagnosed a 'narrow card' bug as a max-width cap on the card vs wider grid cells. The actual root cause was that --min-item-width: 45ch resolved to ~608px in Source Serif at 22px (not the ~450-500px I estimated), so auto-fit collapsed to 1 column. My confidence had been wrong and irritating; the right move would have been to surface uncertainty and ask for a probe.

How to apply: (1) Before proposing a fix for layout issues, get hard numbers — either ask the user to grab devtools computed-style values, or use a headless browser (chrome --headless=new with a probe page or a screenshot, both work and Chrome is installed on Alex's machine). (2) When estimating ch/em/rem-based values, mark the estimate explicitly as 'approximate, please verify' rather than treating it as fact. (3) When the user pushes back on a diagnosis, do not double down — re-examine, ask what info is missing.

# 🚨 NEVER push to main/master. NEVER commit to main/master. NEVER. 🚨

ABSOLUTE RULE — no exceptions, no rationalisations, no "tiny change" carve-outs, no "just this once" reasoning, no historical-pattern excuses.

Every change — including 10-line timestamp updates, .beads/issues.jsonl syncs, doc tweaks, typo fixes, anything at all — goes through:

1. Fresh git worktree (call EnterWorktree).
2. Feature branch.
3. Push the branch with -u.
4. gh pr create (attach skip-version-bump if no shipped code, where applicable).
5. Merge through the GitHub UI / CLI.

Never `git push origin main` (or master). Never `git commit` on main/master directly. Never bypass the PR path "because the diff is trivial". The size of the diff is not a justification — the rule is unconditional.

Why this rule exists: set explicitly by Alex. Repeat-offending on a directly-set rule is the worst possible failure mode in this collaboration — worse than any individual technical mistake. Alex's framing: "you must NEVER NEVER NEVER break rules that I set for you."

How to apply:
- At session-end cleanup time, if there's a tempting "small change on main" to push: STOP. The answer is always "do it through a worktree + PR" or "leave it for the next PR to carry."
- If the rule appears to conflict with another instruction or a historical-pattern observation, the rule wins. Always. Ask Alex if genuinely uncertain — never decide unilaterally.
- If the rule has already been broken, surface it immediately and offer the correct revert path. Don't downplay.
- Re-read this memory at session-close, especially around any cleanup involving .beads/, post-merge sync, or "should I push this small thing."

bd create --deps='blocks:OTHER' direction trap: --deps='blocks:X' on bd create makes the NEW issue block X, not the other way around — opposite of what 'depends' usually means. Workarounds: (1) use plain id with no prefix in --deps (the 'or id' form treats it as 'this issue depends on'), or (2) skip --deps and run 'bd dep <blocker> --blocks <blocked>' after creation. Always verify direction with 'bd dep list <new-issue>' and 'bd ready' immediately after.

Tagging, metadata, and external refs — keep structured info OUT of titles. bd offers three structured-metadata mechanisms with very different filterability. Use the right one so future search/grouping is a one-liner, not a `--title-contains` scan.

- Labels (--labels foo,bar, bd tag <id> <lbl>, bd update --add-label) — flat string tags, multi-valued, filterable through every path: bd list --label X, --label-any X,Y, --label-pattern 'pr-*', --label-regex, AND in bd query "label=X". bd label list-all shows the full namespace. Children inherit parent labels by default (--no-inherit-labels to skip). This is the only mechanism bd query natively understands — make labels the default for anything you'll want to filter on later.
- --external-ref (e.g. gh-45, jira-ABC) — single string per issue, semantic anchor for tooling. NOT queryable via bd query, no native bd list filter — only visible in bd show and JSON export. Use it as a canonical pointer, not as a search axis.
- Metadata (--metadata '{...}', repeatable --set-metadata key=value) — typed key→value JSON. Filterable via bd list --metadata-field key=value and --has-metadata-key=key. Best for values you want to retrieve, not just match (URLs, DOIs, version numbers). Not queryable via bd query.

Conventions (forward-only — don't retrofit existing beads unless asked):

- PR numbers → label `pr-<num>` (e.g. pr-45). Drop [PR #N] title prefixes going forward; the label replaces them. bd query "label=pr-45" answers "all beads from PR 45". bd list --label-pattern 'pr-*' enumerates every PR-tagged bead. Multi-PR beads (review-discovered then fixed in a different PR) carry multiple pr-N labels naturally. Bare digit labels would clash with bead-ID lookups, so always use the `pr-` prefix.
- Optional canonical anchor: also set --external-ref=gh-<num> on the originating PR. Useful for future link-out tooling, but not a substitute — the label is what makes it queryable.
- URLs / DOIs / dashboard links → metadata, keyed by source-type: paper-doi, dashboard-url, gh-issue-url, version-fixed, etc. Surface with bd list --has-metadata-key=paper-doi. Don't bury URLs only in description prose if they should be re-findable.
- Topical area / process state → keep existing labels. Don't invent parallel labels for the same concept; check bd label list-all before coining new ones. Coining a brand-new label for a single bead is rarely worth it — wait until the concept appears in 2+ beads.
- Naming: lowercase, kebab-case, no spaces. Metadata keys same shape (descriptive kebab-case).

Project stack and key paths for alxm.me.

Stack:
- Eleventy static site (Liquid + Nunjucks templates)
- Deployed to Cloudflare Workers + R2 for large assets
- SCSS with CUBE CSS methodology
- Utopia fluid typography and spacing (utopia-core-scss)

Key SCSS paths (verify before relying on these — code is authoritative):
- SCSS entry: src/assets/scss/root.scss
- Compositions: src/assets/scss/compositions/
- Utilities: src/assets/scss/utilities/
- Blocks: src/assets/scss/blocks/
- Design tokens: src/assets/scss/config/_config.scss
- CSS custom properties: src/assets/scss/global/_variables.scss
- Themes (dark mode, artwork): src/assets/scss/global/_themes.scss

When writing code, add tests in the SAME PR for: (a) pure helpers — extract them to module-scope (or a separate file) so they are testable, then test; (b) numeric headline claims in the PR description — pin with `expect(x).toBe(N)`, never `toBeLessThanOrEqual(N)`; (c) integration paths through new state machinery — allocate/grow/write/flush/shift cycles for typed-array buffers, multi-tier reducers, lifecycle FSMs all need a read-back assertion, not just a "does not throw" smoke; (d) auto-upgrade / migration paths (e.g. v2→v3 URL rewrite) that the test plan flags as "manual smoke."

Why: recurring code-review test-gap finding shape. Representative shapes: pseudo-private pure helper not exported (so untestable); PR description claims a specific number ("URL drops to 10 chars") but the test only asserts `≤ 12`. Manual-smoke fallback regresses between releases — automated tests do not.

How to apply: before opening a PR, audit the diff:
1. New function or class? Does each have a test? If pseudo-private, lift to module scope or a `*-pure.ts` sibling.
2. Numbers in the PR title / summary / release notes? Each one must pin with `toBe(N)` somewhere. `toBeLessThanOrEqual` only catches regressions that grow past the bound — not what the headline is selling.
3. New typed-array plumbing (allocate / grow / write / flush / shift)? Add a read-back test that attaches a host with known values, triggers the grow + shift, and asserts the buffer contents at known offsets.
4. Two-tier / N-tier control flow (prime vs fallback, polynomial vs simpler-model)? Each tier must be exercised; the priority semantics must be a separate assertion.
5. PR test plan flags any path as "manual smoke"? Promote the cheapest of those (5–10 line vitest) to automated. Manual smokes are the weakest layer of the regression-prevention strategy.

Canonical form for any multi-line bd write (bd remember, --description, --notes, --design):

    bd remember --key foo "$(cat <<'EOF_INNER'
    body content here, verbatim
    EOF_INNER
    )"

Inside `<<'EOF'` (single-quoted delimiter), the shell does NO backslash interpretation — the body is passed to bd verbatim. Do NOT escape quotes or backticks in the source: a `\"` or `\`` written in the body is stored as the literal two-character sequence (backslash + quote / backslash + backtick), not as `"` or `` ` `` alone. bd does not unescape on store, so the artifacts are visible to every future reader and the memory looks corrupted.

Symptom: previewing with `bd memories <key> --json` shows `\"` or `\`` where you wanted plain `"` or `` ` ``.

After any multi-line write, sanity-check by reading the body back. Cheap; protects against silent corruption no test catches:

    bd memories <key> --json | python3 -c "import json,sys; print(json.load(sys.stdin).get('<key>',''))" | head -40

Same rule applies to bd create --description=, bd update --notes=, bd update --design=, and any other multi-line content routed through the same heredoc form.

Concurrent Claude sessions: bd issues marked in_progress with matching uncommitted edits in the worktree usually indicate another live session is working on that bead. Do NOT pick it up, run quality gates against it, stage/commit/push, or close the bead. Confirm with the user first. The Status: in_progress field is the canonical 'someone else has this' signal — git branch ownership alone is not enough because branches are shared across sessions.

Don't rm -rf _site during a live dev preview. Why: it kills the running 11ty dev server's served assets, breaks any browser tabs the user has open against the preview, and forces a cold rebuild. How to apply: if a stale build is suspected during 'pnpm run 11ty:watch', stop the watcher first (or 'pnpm run clean' between runs, which is fine before starting the watcher) — never delete _site while the watcher is live. Same applies if any other dev preview is serving files out of _site/.

For large multi-bead PRs (~10+ issues in one PR) Alex authored, proactively distinguish code with strong unit-test coverage from code that requires manual verification. Flag specific test-plan gaps rather than implying overall confidence.

Confidence categories at PR-open time:
- High: changed code paths exercised by unit/integration tests.
- Medium: tests cover adjacent code but not the integration point.
- Low (needs eyeballs): user-visible paths with only unit-level coverage; constructor signature changes; callback rewiring; build-pipeline scripts; generated artifacts.

How to apply at PR-open time:
- Audit the manual-smoke checklist against the actual diff.
- Add an explicit smoke step for any user-visible path with only unit-level coverage.
- Refactors that change a constructor signature or callback wiring always get a smoke step.
- Scripts/pipelines need at least a "run it and check the log line" smoke.
- Distinguish "tests pass" from "behavior verified" in the PR description so reviewers can prioritize their manual passes.

CSS Architecture for alxm.me. Core approach: CUBE CSS + Every Layout primitives + Utopia fluid type/space.

Rules:
- NEVER hardcode values. Always stay within the design token system.
- CSS values must use CSS custom properties (--flow-space, --gutter, etc.) or SCSS helpers (dt.get-size(), dt.get-color(), dt.use(), dt.get-value()).
- Exceptions (state variations) use data-* attributes, never additional CSS classes.
- HTML class grouping: class="[ block ] [ composition ] [ utilities ]" with square brackets.
- First reach for an existing composition or utility before writing new CSS.

There is a 'cube-css' skill in this repository — invoke it via the Skill tool when making any CSS/SCSS or font changes. Read it before making changes.

How Alex likes bd data (memories + issues) groomed for context efficiency and prioritised execution.

Two paired passes — memory pruning and issue grooming — both about signal-to-noise: memories load every session, and open issues drive 'bd ready'. Bloat in either silently degrades how useful bd is.

Why: Memory bloat dilutes session context. Mis-prioritised or cluttered open issues break the prioritisation framework. Both compound between grooming passes if not maintained.

How to apply: Run grooming when Alex asks ("prune memories", "groom beads", "check what's worth retiring", or similar). Survey first, present a categorized plan, wait for approval, then execute mechanically in batches.

=== Memory pruning ===

Categorize each into drop / trim / consolidate / keep.

- Drop when content lives elsewhere: a relevant bead (search bd first — bead descriptions often have richer context than the memory), canonical docs (CLAUDE.md, RELEASING.md, docs/*, project reference docs), time-bound notes aged out without follow-up, or lessons from abandoned PRs once the conclusion is settled product policy.
- Trim by keeping rule + why + how-to-apply; drop historical incident logs, exact dates, sub-task IDs, file:line paths that belong in code comments. For memories that group rules into shapes, keep one representative example per shape — not the full enumeration.
- Consolidate multiple memories on the same operational area with non-overlapping scope into one structured memory with sections.
- Move long technical content (invariants, file paths, debug HUDs) into the project's architecture/docs and a code comment at the call site, then drop the memory.

Decision rule: if it's not relevant to every session, it doesn't belong in memory. Beads/docs are the right home for area-specific content.

=== Bead grooming ===

Survey via 'bd list --status=open --limit=0 --json'; the tree output paginates and is hard to read at scale, so script the grouping with python (parent/child via dependencies[].type='parent' or 'parent-child').

Categories:
1. Duplicates — close with bd close <id> --reason='duplicate of <other>'.
2. Strays under existing epics — reparent with 'bd update <id> --parent <epic>'.
3. New epics — when 3+ coupled beads should land together; create the epic and call out the design-gate child in the description.
4. Orphaned children from closed epics — reparent to the surviving parent.
5. Re-prioritisation against the project's prioritization framework:
   - Research / "investigate" tasks at P1 → P2 (P1 is for in-flight rewrites, not upstream research)
   - Code-quality children default to P3 unless coupled to in-flight P1/P2 work
6. Defer candidates — 'bd defer <ids...>' (status-based, no --until needed). Common categories Alex tends to defer:
   - Mobile work, until desktop is solid
   - Public site / FAQ / marketing, until ready to ship
   - Far-out / low-impact features
   - Polish that isn't on the critical path
   - Small UX decisions filed for "someday" (defer until they actually matter)

Specific judgment calls worth asking Alex about before executing: research-stage P1 → P2 transitions, whether specific cleanup tasks are still live or now obsolete, and whether to defer entire epics vs just children.

Verify with 'bd ready' post-grooming — should be meaningfully shorter and reflect actual scope.

Related project: alexmarshall.me is a counselling-focused fork of alxm.me, created 2026-03-17.

Repo: https://github.com/alexmensch/alexmarshall.me (public)

Key differences from alxm.me (verify against current code — fork may have diverged further):
- Counselling landing page as homepage (permalink /)
- Writing filtered to psychology-tagged articles only via pagination.before in writing.11tydata.js (checks item.tags)
- Writing articles have canonical URLs pointing to alxm.me
- Podcast is a simple platform links page (no RSS/episodes/audio)
- Guided inner journey exercises at /exercises/guided-journeys/ with R2-hosted MP3s
- Removed: artwork, about, advisory, OG images, syntax highlighting, permalink tracking, audio validation, LLMs.txt, Atom feed, loremIpsum
- Simplified worker (R2 + static assets, no RSS handling)
- Company info always shown in footer (no conditional)
- SCSS cascade order fixed: compositions → utilities → blocks

Why: User wants a separate, focused counselling practice website at alexmarshall.me.

How to apply: When working on either site, be aware they share the same Cloudflare KV namespace for writing content but are otherwise independent repos.

SCSS cascade order rule: in src/assets/scss/root.scss, imports must be ordered compositions → utilities → blocks.

Why: Per CUBE CSS methodology, blocks refine compositions and must appear later in the cascade. PR #214 (alxm.me) fixed this — the previous order (utilities → blocks → compositions) only worked by accident because _artwork.scss transitively loaded compositions early via @use "../compositions". This caused .box { outline: transparent } to override .contact-options { outline-color } on the alexmarshall.me fork (which removed _artwork.scss).

How to apply: When making SCSS changes to either alxm.me or alexmarshall.me, ensure the cascade order in root.scss is respected: compositions before utilities before blocks. Reorder if a refactor breaks it.

Project policy for task tracking — overrides bd prime's built-in "Prohibited: Do NOT use TodoWrite, TaskCreate, or markdown files for task tracking" Core Rule. The bd built-in is too strict; the split below is the actual policy.

The split:

- Beads is the canonical tracker for anything that should outlive this session — new tasks, bugs, refactors, follow-ups, design decisions. Create a bead BEFORE writing code, mark in_progress when starting, close when shipped.

- TaskCreate / TodoWrite is allowed for in-session step decomposition — discrete sub-steps of an in-flight bead too granular to file individually and with no value next session (e.g. "update import in file X", "regen catalog", "run vitest", "open PR"). The bead is the unit of persistent work; the task list is a working surface for executing it.

- Tie-break: when in doubt, file a bead. Persistence you don't need beats lost context. If a sub-step turns out to be its own piece of follow-up work, promote it to a bead and close the task entry.

- Never use markdown TODO files (TODO.md, NOTES.md, etc.) — they decay silently and aren't searchable.

Why: The bd built-in rule conflates "ephemeral tracking is wasteful when work outlives the session" (true) with "ephemeral tracking is bad in itself" (not true). TaskCreate has a real job — a live ticking checklist for multi-step work happening in this session — that beads doesn't do well, and a bead per micro-step is noise in bd ready and noise in the JSONL diff.

Lives as a memory rather than in CLAUDE.md because the Beads section of CLAUDE.md is inside an auto-managed BEGIN BEADS INTEGRATION block — easily clobbered on bd integrate refresh. Memory survives.

How to apply:
- Default: file a bead. TaskCreate is for the sub-steps INSIDE an in-flight bead, not for the principal work.
- Single-step changes (one-line edit, one quick fix, a docs typo) need neither — just do them.
- If a sub-step in the TaskCreate list grows into "needs more thought / its own commit / cross-session work", lift it to a bead immediately and remove the task entry.
- Do not invent parallel TaskCreate lists for work that already has a bead — the bead's notes / description carries that record.

How to scope a new bead so Alex doesn't have to ask for a re-split later.

The rule: Each bead = one commit/PR + a stated smoke/test step. Sized to fit a compact context window so it can be tackled with a session reset between beads. If the work is bigger, file an epic with children at creation time — not a single bead that needs splitting later.

Why: Bigger beads bundle multiple commits, force session resets mid-bead, and make review noisier. Doing this at creation time saves Alex from asking for a re-split.

How to apply — natural seams to decompose along:

Before filing, ask: would this be one commit + one smoke step, or several? If several, file an epic with children. Common seams:

- Research / decision → implementation. If the bead has "decide X" or "evaluate Y" before code lands, split. Research is a written-output deliverable; implementation depends on its conclusion.
- Data pipeline → runtime. Build script + output artifact is one commit; consumer / runtime loader is another. Pipeline ships independently; runtime tests against committed artifacts.
- Independent affordances bundled together. When the description says "two related X filed in one issue," or enumerates independent features, file each as its own bead.
- Distinct sub-system additions, even if they share a parent epic.
- Per-source / per-type ingest, only if each source is independently shippable and useful — don't split if the value is the unified output.

Don't decompose when work is genuinely indivisible: a single bug fix, a focused refactor, a decision producing one output, a small UX tweak that's one DOM/CSS change.

Wiring at creation time:
- Convert parent to --type=epic when it acquires children.
- Keep the parent's master spec in its description; children carry focused per-task scope + acceptance criteria + smoke step.
- Use bd dep <depender> <blocker> between siblings to express ordering (research blocks impl, pipeline blocks runtime). Use --type=related for cross-cutting non-blocking relationships. Never rely on prose "see issue X" — Alex plans from the dependency graph.
- If parent is deferred, defer children too via bd defer.
- Verify with bd dep list <new-issue> + bd ready immediately after — direction is the common landmine (see bd-create-deps-blocks-direction-trap).

Prefer small topical commits: when a change touches multiple concerns, split them into separate commits each with a focused subject line and brief why-body.

Why: validated previously — splitting unrelated work into separate commits was accepted without pushback. One-concept-per-commit keeps the repo's story legible and makes revert/bisect surgical.

How to apply: when there are unrelated staged changes, don't reach for one omnibus commit message — stage and commit each concern separately. Err toward more commits, not fewer. Keep using HEREDOC commit format with the Co-Authored-By: Claude trailer.

When a PR is framed as "apply pattern X to all the Y in this layer" (every overlay, every event handler, every picker entry point, every shader pass, every DRY blend), enumerate the set of Y EXPLICITLY in the PR description AND verify the implementation covers each. One missed peer = the PR headline claim is false.

Why: recurring code-review finding shape across multiple PRs. Representative example: a PR claimed "all five remaining overlay modules" got dirty-tracking but a sixth peer was missed (and the most-instantiated one in the hot path) — the headline claim was false because one peer was missed. Pattern divergence in the same layer is exactly what "architectural fit" reviews catch — much cheaper to fix at write time than at retrospective.

How to apply:
1. Before starting the refactor, write the explicit list of peers in the PR description. Skim CLAUDE.md's module roster for the cross-cutting layer being touched (overlays, picker, event handlers, debug-panel sections, shaders, etc.) — it is the canonical list of peers.
2. After implementing, grep for the OLD pattern (the thing being replaced) and confirm ZERO remaining call sites in scope. If non-zero, either convert them or call them out as "deliberately deferred" with a follow-up bead.
3. If two peers in the same layer end up with two strategies (e.g. per-attribute dirty-track vs whole-frame signature dirty-track), document the chosen strategy in the layer-canonical doc and pick one for the layer or reconcile.
4. Sister-layer extension — when extending a feature for one host, check whether sibling hosts have the same surface and would benefit / drift if not extended too. File a bead for the sibling work even if out of scope.

Don't loop on failed web fetches. If a URL access fails (404, blocked, auth wall, AI-scraper filter, etc.), do NOT keep trying alternative transports (WebFetch -> curl -> different URL -> etc.) in a loop.

Why: the user flagged this explicitly — they don't want time and tokens burned on transport attempts that aren't converging. It's faster for them to grab it manually than to thrash.

How to apply: on the first failure, try one alternative. On the second failure, stop and ask the user to fetch the content themselves and paste it in (or drop the file into the repo, or give the information directly). Tell them the exact URL being attempted so they can fetch it with minimal context-switching.

How to author and maintain bd content well across sessions.

(1) Prefer formal bd dep links over prose cross-references whenever the relationship affects planning, ordering, or future work decisions. Alex plans from the dependency graph (bd ready, bd dep list, bd show) — prose 'see issue X' references don't surface there and are easy to miss. Use 'bd dep add' for blocks, the relate variant for relates_to, --parent for parent-child. After filing a batch of issues, audit own descriptions for cross-reference prose and convert each to a formal link before declaring done. Use 'relate' for cross-epic connections (bd's blocks between epics often errors). Avoid epic-level blocks when task-level deps can express the same constraint, since open parents with inbound blockers cause bd ready to cascade-block all children.

(2) When you notice a bd memory is stale, deficient, or contradicts current repo state, update it via 'bd remember --key <existing-key> ...' immediately rather than just noting it in chat. Why: Alex wants memories actively maintained — drift compounds across sessions if corrections aren't written back, and the next session reads the stale version. How to apply: when reading a memory whose specifics don't match current code/config (referenced file no longer exists, flag renamed, policy changed, commit hash referenced is gone, etc.), re-author the memory body in place. Rewrite the whole body each time so it stays self-contained; fragmented amendments are hard to read. Same applies to memories about commands, paths, and project structure.

How bd state persists in this project — two related operational facts:

(1) bd state is shared across all git worktrees. Running bd commands from a .claude/worktrees/* checkout writes to the same database the main checkout reads from. The .beads/issues.jsonl file in any worktree is a stale snapshot from worktree creation, NOT the live source of truth — don't be alarmed when it doesn't change after bd writes; don't redo bd commands; don't go searching for DB paths. Trust bd's exit code: success means persisted globally.

(2) 'bd dolt push' is a no-op here — no Dolt remote is configured (the embedded dolt at .beads/embeddeddolt has no upstream; verify with 'bd dolt remote list'). The git-tracked .beads/issues.jsonl is the source of truth and propagates via normal 'git push'. Skip 'bd dolt push' in the session-close workflow for this project; do not treat its informational 'Supported remote URLs' output as needing fixing. The CLAUDE.md project workflow lists 'bd dolt push' generically — that's fine to leave as documentation of the standard pattern, but in practice for alxm.me only 'git push' is needed for beads to propagate.

Defer doc updates until the final commit. Don't edit CLAUDE.md, README.md, or anything under product/ or docs/ while implementing a change — treat code changes as the only deliverable until commit time.

Why: mid-session doc edits become churn — direction shifts, features get dropped, parameters rename, knob values move; the paragraph written early ends up describing something that no longer exists.

How to apply: during implementation confine edits to code, templates, styles, tests; if a code comment references a doc section about to be wrong, leave it and surface at commit time. At commit time, grep the final diff for renames, removed knobs, new behavior, anything user-visible — open CLAUDE.md, README.md, product/, docs/ and update only what's now stale. Skip ones that don't need changes.
